author: @RandomSmith
text: Where you were connecting a single switch, you could have a circular arrangement of 3 contacts with gaps on them, then spin a handle over multiple revolutions, each revolution would hit each of the 3 switches (ie join the gap to connect both parts of the “switch wire”) in turn. You could control the speed at which you turn the handle and if you get it right , the motor would spin faster, a manual speed controller so to speak

author: @davidprock904
text:
Mr Smith, could you please find out what happens when you use graphite rod(s) as the core for a transformer? 
Keep in mind graphite is diamagnetic, there is many other reasons I wonder, I plan on designing a starship coil transformer where I preform electrolysis with the core as the electrodes.
Remember your video bringing a magnet up to hho production increase the performance... with the setup mentioned it will piggyback off the magnetics in the transformer.
And if you allow the starship coil to spin the rods in place, or even spinning on a fixture maybe, but in place would be better, the bubbles won't be clinging around on the rods improving the preference.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
I think your lab would benefit from having a programmable three phase supply, if you are going to continue experimenting with motors. One possible solution might be to use an Arduino to generate a variable frequency three phase sign wave output and feed this to three audio power amplifier ICs, say 20 watts each.
Alternatively, just use the microcontroller  signals to drive three H bridge ICs, then at least you could replace the switches with a drive signal.

author: @C-M-E
text:
Rob, completed unrelated to the vid, but you are absolutely the first person I thought of to share this with for confirmation. So I've been working with graphite and some other powdered materials for UHTC research and currently I'm treating them with 85% phosphoric acid baths, then tried the sonification idea for intercalation purposes after watching one of your older videos earlier this week.
Today I also tried MEK instead of Acetone as I was out. Didn't seem to work, however I did have a surprise result moments ago. After filtering the MEK sonicated batch through a coffee filter that happened to drop down into the same cup I caught the drippings of the phosphoric acid, I couldn't believe what I was seeing. As the MEK dripped into the cup, it was PULLING the graphite/graphene out of the solution and collecting along the top of the surface, very much like Mercury pooling together!! 
I then dripped a few more out of my MEK sonificated batch and holy cow, it was like MAGIC! I'm about to head back down to the shed with a recorder. 
Any thoughts?

author: @lightcapmath2777
text: excellent! will there be any problems with eddy currents? David

replies.author: @MrTubeuser12
replies.text: shouldn't be an issue, this is a reluctance motor which doesn't produce eddy current's by virtue of it's design,(one of it's advantages), usually eddy currents come about because of a permanent magnetic field, however being able to "switch off" the permanent magnet with the coils should all but eliminate that effect, that said, I noticed in this design 1 phase still has a small magnetic field at any given moment so that could be an issue.

replies.author: @lightcapmath2777

replies.author: @edlubs11
replies.text:
@rogerbraintree9552 it's an incredibly scalable brushless motor!!!! Holy cow!! With a simple bldc controller, like ones used in quad copters, you could make incredibly cheap motors that are also incredibly light weight. 

Some design work to introduce a gear box and we have small servos like those found in robotic arms. I have no idea why it took me three years to find this but I'm glad I did. It's brilliant!
